nativeRange.habitat: Shallow tropical and subtropical reef flats, reef crests, and rubble zones; typically found in well-lit, moderate-flow areas from the intertidal zone to moderate depths

decorPreferences: spacing from neighboring corals to limit allelopathic spread

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae. Tend to benefit from weekly target feeding of meaty foods such as Mysis shrimp, finely chopped marine flesh, zooplankton, or commercial coral foods. Will also absorb dissolved nutrients from the water column.

avoidWith: neighboring corals placed in direct contact — Palythoa encrusts and will overgrow them
predatory snails such as box snails (known to prey on polyps)

summary: Palythoa are large-polyp encrusting zoanthids found across tropical reefs worldwide. Hardy and colorful, they are generally considered beginner-friendly for husbandry — but elevated to intermediate care overall due to palytoxin handling risks.
careNotes:
SAFETY WARNING — PALYTOXIN: Palythoa corals are well-documented to contain palytoxin, one of the most potent non-protein toxins known to science. Not every colony or species carries it in equal amounts, but the risk is real and must be treated seriously.
• Always wear nitrile or latex gloves AND eye protection (splash goggles, not just glasses) when handling Palythoa, fragging, or doing any maintenance that disturbs the colony.
• NEVER heat, boil, or cook live rock, substrate, or water that may contain Palythoa fragments. Aerosolized palytoxin has caused documented poisonings, hospitalizations, and multiple reported fatalities among home reefkeepers. Even small fragments heated in tap water have been sufficient to cause severe respiratory distress.
• Wash hands thoroughly after any handling even if gloves were worn.
• If palytoxin exposure is suspected (skin rash, eye irritation, tingling, respiratory distress, nausea), seek medical attention immediately and inform treating physicians of palytoxin exposure — it is not commonly known in emergency medicine.

Husbandry notes:
• Palythoa is a large genus (100+ species) commonly sold under the genus name in the aquarium trade. All species share broadly similar husbandry requirements, though polyp size, color, and growth rate vary considerably.
• Colony growth: Under favorable conditions, Palythoa tend to encrust and spread rapidly across available rock surface. They have been known to overgrow neighboring corals if not given adequate spacing. Plan for ongoing fragging/pruning to control spread.
• Allelopathy: Like most zoanthids, Palythoa can release chemical compounds into the water that may stress neighboring corals. Regular activated carbon or good protein skimming is generally advised in mixed-coral systems.
• Lighting acclimation: New colonies should be introduced to the target PAR range gradually — starting lower in the tank and moving upward over 2–4 weeks helps prevent bleaching from light shock.
• Flow: Moderate indirect flow is generally preferred. Direct, high-velocity flow aimed at the polyps may cause them to remain closed. Flow helps carry food particles to the polyps and prevents detritus accumulation.
• Feeding: Although primarily photosynthetic, Palythoa have been documented to respond positively to weekly meaty target feeding (Mysis, chopped marine flesh, zooplankton). Feeding tends to accelerate growth and improve coloration.
• Placement: As encrusting corals, Palythoa tend to do well in almost any tank position; middle placement at moderate PAR and flow is typical. Avoid placing them in direct contact with SPS or LPS corals.
• Tank size: Palythoa are commonly kept in nano and pico reef systems. The 76 L (20 gallon) minimum reflects typical nano reef practice; smaller systems are sometimes used but require more frequent water changes and careful attention to nutrient stability.
• Reproduction: Palythoa tend to reproduce readily by budding in established reef tanks. Fragging (cutting) is also straightforward and widely practiced. Fraggers must wear full PPE due to palytoxin risk.
breedingNotes: Palythoa reproduce readily in captivity by budding — new polyps emerge from the mat (coenenchyme) surrounding existing polyps, gradually expanding the colony footprint. Sexual reproduction via broadcast spawning occurs on natural reefs but is not deliberately practiced in home aquaria. Fragging (cutting) with bone snips or razor blades is the standard propagation method among hobbyists; PPE (gloves, eye protection) is essential during any fragging procedure due to palytoxin risk.

primary.name: LiveAquaria — Button Polyp, Red People Eater (Palythoa sp.)
primary.notes: Primary product page for Palythoa sp. on LiveAquaria. Provides: temperature 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, salinity 1.023–1.025, kH 8–12, lighting moderate, flow medium, placement middle, care level easy, temperament semi-aggressive.

additional.name: Reef Builders — Zoanthid & Palythoa Intro Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters: temperature 75–78°F, salinity 1.025–1.026, kH 8–12, Ca 400–450 ppm, Mg 1250–1350 ppm. PAR 50–150. Feeding: twice weekly. Confirmed palytoxin safety warnings.

additional.name: Reef Chasers — Zoanthids & Palythoa Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked: temperature 77–84°F, pH 8.0–8.4, kH 8–9, Ca 400–450 ppm, PAR 150–200 (moderate), flow moderate-high indirect, care level beginner. Palytoxin described as 'one of the most lethal toxins on the planet'; boiling rock explicitly warned against.

additional.name: Plankton Marin — Comprehensive Guide to Palythoa Corals
additional.notes: Cross-checked: temperature 75–82°F, pH 8.1–8.4, salinity 1.023–1.025, care level low-maintenance/beginner, native range Pacific and Indian Oceans, family Zoanthidae (note: Wikipedia and WoRMS list family Sphenopidae; Zoanthidae is sometimes used informally). Confirmed palytoxin warnings requiring gloves and eye protection.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Palythoa (taxonomy and palytoxin)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy confirmation: Order Zoantharia, Family Sphenopidae, genus Palythoa Lamouroux 1816. Confirmed palytoxin presence in many (not all) members. Not used for husbandry parameters.
